coredata.dc:title: Dual-mode turn-on ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on carbon dots and CuInS2/ZnS quantum dots for detection of chlorotetracycline
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xiaoping
Lin, Jianwei
Zhuang, Yafeng
Huang, Siqi
Chen, Jinghua
Han, Zhizhong
coredata.dc:description: A new ratiometric fluorescence sensor is prepared for selective detection of chlorotetracycline (CTC) through dual-mode fluorescence method. The sensor is composed of carbon dots (CDs) with blue emission and carboxyl-modified CuInS2/ZnS quantum dots (QDs) with dark-red emission. Usually QDs are used as fluorescent probes or signal sources, but it is interesting in this strategy that CuInS2/ZnS QDs innovatively work as quenching agent to reduce the fluorescence of CDs, mainly due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). After the addition of CTC, the interaction between CDs and CuInS2/ZnS QDs is restrained, resulting in the fluorescence recovery of CDs, whilstthe QDs’ fluorescence remains unaffected. In this work, CTC is detected in the range of 0–50 μM by conventional fluorescence and synchronous fluorescence methods under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm or Δλ = 90 nm, and the detection limits of the two methods are 0.46 μM and 0.36 μM, respectively. The designed sensor displays good selectivity compared with other tetracycline drugs with similar structure to CTC, different ions and various natural - amino acids. And the sensor can also be applied to determine CTC in tap water and milk.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Quantum dots
Ratiometric fluorescence
Synchronous fluorescence
Chlorotetracycline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs and CuInS2/ZnS QDs
Characterization of carbon dots and CuInS2/ZnS quantum dots
Selectivity of the CDs-CuInS2/ZnS QDs based fluorescence sensor
Detection of CTC in actual samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of CDs. Insert: images of CDs under UV lamp. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of CuInS2/ZnS QDs. Insert: images of CuInS2/ZnS QDs (right) and MPA-CuInS2/ZnS QDs (left) under UV lamp.

(A) Wavelength difference selection of synchronous fluorescence. The influence of incubation time on fluorescence ratio: (B) fluorescence, (C) synchronous fluorescence. Fixed concentration of CDs, fluorescence ratio with different concentrations of QDs: (D) fluorescence, (E) synchronous fluorescence. With different pH, the fluorescence ratio in the presence of CTC solution: (F) fluorescence, (G) synchronous fluorescence.

Fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of CTC: (A) Fluorescence emission spectra, (B) Synchronous fluorescence spectrum. Effects of interfering substances: (C) Fluorescence, (D) Synchronous fluorescence.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of QDs and fluorescence spectra of CDs. (B) The decay curves of CDs and CDs-QDs.
Schematic illustration of the designed fluorescence sensor for ratiometric detection of CTC.
CTC detection in water and milk by the developed fluorescence method.
CTC detection in water and milk by the developed synchronous fluorescence method.
Comparison of different methods for determination of CTC.

head.ce:title: Dual-mode turn-on ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on carbon dots and CuInS2/ZnS quantum dots for detection of chlorotetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350122, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350122, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Key Laboratory of Drug Target Discovery and Structural and Functional Research, Fuzhou 350122, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Key Laboratory of Drug Target Discovery and Structural and Functional Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fujian Key Laboratory of Drug Target Discovery and Structural and Functional Research, Fuzhou 350122, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350122, PR China (Z. Han).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new ratiometric fluorescence sensor is prepared for selective detection of chlorotetracycline (CTC) through dual-mode fluorescence method. The sensor is composed of carbon dots (CDs) with blue emission and carboxyl-modified CuInS2/ZnS quantum dots (QDs) with dark-red emission. Usually QDs are used as fluorescent probes or signal sources, but it is interesting in this strategy that CuInS2/ZnS QDs innovatively work as quenching agent to reduce the fluorescence of CDs, mainly due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). After the addition of CTC, the interaction between CDs and CuInS2/ZnS QDs is restrained, resulting in the fluorescence recovery of CDs, whilstthe QDs’ fluorescence remains unaffected. In this work, CTC is detected in the range of 0–50 μM by conventional fluorescence and synchronous fluorescence methods under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm or Δλ = 90 nm, and the detection limits of the two methods are 0.46 μM and 0.36 μM, respectively. The designed sensor displays good selectivity compared with other tetracycline drugs with similar structure to CTC, different ions and various natural - amino acids. And the sensor can also be applied to determine CTC in tap water and milk.

ce:section.ce:para: Chlorotetracycline (CTC) is a broad - spectrum tetracycline antibiotic, which can be used to treat diseases, and also promote growth and prevent CTC-sensitive bacteria in animal husbandry [1]. However, the abuse of CTC has certain harm. For example, CTC will eventually accumulate in the human body along the food chain, causing a certain threat to human health. Therefore, it is necessary to establish a rapid and accurate method to detect CTC.

Up to now, a series of analytical methods have been used for the detection of CTC, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [2,3], electrophoresis [4] and electrochemical methods [5,6]. So far, fluorescence detection with the advantages of high specificity, low cost and high sensitivity has been successfully used in CTC measurement [7,8]. However, the previous fluorescence sensors were mainly based on fluorescence quenching mechanism for CTC detection, while the “off–on” mechanism is used for detection of CTC in this paper. Among fluorescence methods, synchronous fluorescence method is widely used because of its advantages such as high resolution, reducing the scattering of different wavelengths and avoiding the interference of some peaks in the conventional fluorescence spectrum [9]. Therefore, in order to reduce the interference of some peaks and distinguish tetracycline drugs, it may be a unique way to construct a dual-mode CTC detection method with conventional fluorescence and synchronous fluorescence.

For fluorescence detection of CTC, most of them are single-emission fluorescence response, and their signals are easily interfered by sensor concentration, noise and other factors, which limit their sensitivity. While ratiometric fluorescence has self-calibration ability, and it can improve the sensitivity and selectivity of detection [10,11]. Several materials are used for ratiometric fluorescence detection, such as quantum dots (QDs), organic dyes and so on. Semiconductor QDs are widely used as fluorescent probes due to their unique optical and photoelectric properties, such as wide UV–vis absorption spectrum, anti-bleaching, and high quantum yield [12,13]. Carbon dots (CDs) are characterized by good water solubility, tunable optical properties [14], strong light stability [15–17], low toxicity [18] and simple and economical synthesis methods [19], which make them have a broad application prospect in fluorescent sensors.

Herein, we prepared a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on CDs-CuInS2/ZnS QDs, which detected CTC using synchronous fluorescence and fluorescence method. In addition, the advantages and disadvantages of the two detection methods were compared. The designed sensor is easy to prepare and has good specificity, and the fluorescence responses of oxytetracycline (OTC), tetracycline (TC) and other chloramphenicol analogue, which have the similar structure with CTC, are different from that of CTC.

Citric acid (99.5%), Polyvinyl polyamine, Polyoxyethylene bis(amine) and Chlorotetracycline hydrochloride (C22H23ClN2O8·HCl, C), Ceftriaxone disodium salt hemiheptahydrate (C18H16N8Na2O7S3, 98%,Cro) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Copper(II) chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O) is purachased from Shanghai Experimental Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Trisodium citrate dihydrate(C6H5Na3O7), L-Cysteine(C3H7NO2S) and D-(+)-Glucose(C6H12O5·H2O) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Indium chloride tetrahydrate (InCl3·4H2O), Sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S·9H2O), Thiourea (CH4N2S, 99%), Zinc acetate (C4H6O4Zn·2H2O, 99.0%), Glutathione (Reduced) (C10H17N3O6S, 98%), L-Lysine (C6H14N2O2, Lys), L-Arginine (C6H14N4O2, Arg), L-Histidine (C6H9N3O2, 99%, His), Tetracycline hydrochloride (C22H24N2O8·HCl, TC) and Glutamic acid (C5H9NO4, Glu) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Glycine (C2H6NO2, Gly) and Terramycin (C22H24N2O9, OTC) were purchased from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). 3-Mercaptopropionic Acid (C3H6O2S, MPA) was purchased from TCI (Shanghai) Development Co., Ltd (Tokyo, Japan).

The fluorescent spectra were recorded by an Agilent Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer. The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Gensys 150 spectrophotometer. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were taken on an FEI Talos F200s transmission electron microscope. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was taken with a Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha+ X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The fluorescence lifetime was collected by an Edinburgh FS5 spectrofluorometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and CuInS2/ZnS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis procedure of CDs emitting blue light and CuInS2/ZnS QDs emitting red light were described in supplementary materials. And using the fluorescence quantum yield of quinine sulfate at 0.1 M H2SO4 (Q R = 0.54) as the standard, the fluorescence emission spectrum was measured at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, so as to measure the relative fluorescence quantum yield of CDs.

Fluorescence measurement of CTC was accomplished using the following steps. First, 88 μL of MPA-CuInS2/ZnS QDs and 20 μL of CDs diluted 200 times were mixed to construct the probe. Subsequently, 20 μL CTC with different concentrations was added. Then, the mixtures were diluted to 200 μL with pH 11.0 PB (10 mmol·L-1) and mixed thoroughly. Finally, the fluorescence spectra of above mixture were recorded with excitation at 360 nm and the synchronous fluorescence spectra were recorded at wavelength difference of 90 nm and range of 200–800 nm. We studied the relation of ΔI R and concentration of CTC. ΔI R = I CDs/I QDs - I0
CDs/I0
QDs, where the I0
CDs, I0
QDs were the fluorescence intensity of CDs and QDs without CTC (Conventional fluorescence was ΔI R1, synchronous fluorescence was ΔI R2).

To further investigate the selectivity of this CDs-CuInS2/ZnS QDs system, the fluorescence response of the sensor toward some interfering substances were measured, including common inorganic ions, representative amino acids, glucose and some tetracycline drugs with similar structures to CTC. The selectivity tests were carried out under the same detection conditions as the detection of CTC.

The milk was purchased from a local supermarket. In order to remove the proteins and lipids in milk, according to the practice in the literature, 1% trichloroacetic acid was added to the milk sample and mixed in a 10 mL centrifuge tube at a 1:1 vol After mixing, the proteins were removed by centrifugation at 10,000 r/min for 10 min, and the precipitation was finally removed by a 0.22 µm filter membrane [20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carbon dots and CuInS2/ZnS quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in TEM and AFM diagrams of CDs (Fig. S1A, S1B), the average particle size of CDs is 5 nm and the average thickness is 2 nm. As shown in TEM of CuInS2/ZnS QDs (Fig. S1C), the average particle size is 3 nm. The results indicate the successful preparation of CDs and CuInS2/ZnS QDs. And the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs is 31.8% (Fig. S2A). The FTIR (Fig. S2B) and XPS (Fig. S3) results demonstrate that carboxyl and amino groups do exist on the CDs, so the CDs have excellent water solubility, which is beneficial to the further modification. The specific description is mentioned in supplementary materials.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs diluted 100 times shows two obvious absorption peaks at 240 nm and 354 nm (Fig. 1 A). The fluorescence spectra display that the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs are located at 350 nm and 438 nm, respectively. The inset in Fig. 1A shows an image of CDs illuminated by a 365 nm UV lamp, which reveals a strong blue fluorescence.

As shown in Fig. 1B, CuInS2/ZnS QDs have an absorption region at 300–800 nm. At the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the maximum emission wavelength of QDs is located at 668 nm. After modifying CuInS2/ZnS QDs by MPA, the maximum emission peak of QDs is red-shifted to 682 nm and the fluorescence intensity decreases. The inset in Fig. 1B shows an image of a dispersion of CuInS2/ZnS QDs and MPA-CuInS2/ZnS QDs in water illuminated by a 365 nm UV lamp.

In order to obtain accurate and stable results, the wavelength difference of synchronous fluorescence, reaction time, QDs concentration and pH were investigated. Synchronous fluorescence with wavelength difference from 50 to 100 nm was measured, the fluorescence intensity of QDs and CDs reach the highest when the wavelength difference is 90 nm (Fig. 2 A). After addition of CTC, the fluorescence response of the sensing system was recorded over time. The results show that the I CDs/I QDs reaches the highest value at 40 min and tend to be stable after 40 min (Fig. 2B and 2C). Next, we fix the concentration of CDs at 4.27 μg·mL−1 and change the concentration of QDs. As can be seen from the results of Fig. 2D and 2E, the fluorescence intensity of CDs reduces with the increase of the concentration of QDs. The difference is that the fluorescence intensity of QDs increases in the synchronous fluorescence spectra. This is mainly because the conventional fluorescence analysis method would have a “self-extinguishing” effect when the solution concentration is too large, and the solution would absorb the excitation light near the absorption of the solution, then the emission intensity would decrease, resulting in the emission intensity is not proportional to the concentration [21]. Therefore, in order to achieve maximum fluorescence recovery efficiency and maintain consistency, 0.7 mM QDs and 4.27 μg·mL−1 CDs are selected to construct the fluorescence proportional sensor. Finally, the influence of pH on the fluorescence response of the sensor is investigated. When pH is 11, the fluorescence intensity ratio reaches the maximum (Fig. 2F and 2G). Therefore, we determine that the wavelength difference is 90 nm, the concentration of QDs is 0.7 mM, the CDs concentration is 4.27 μg·mL−1, the pH value is 11 and the reaction time is 40 min as the final conditions.

Under the optimized experimental conditions, the response of the sensing system to CTC was studied by fluorescence and synchronous fluorescence methods. After added different concentrations of CTC solutions, the fluorescence intensity of CDs increases with the increase of CTC concentration and the peak shifts blue, while the fluorescence intensity of QDs remains ignorable change (Fig. 3 A and 3B). There is a good linear relationship between ΔI R1 of conventional fluorescence and CTC concentration in the range of 1–70 μM, while there is a good linear relationship between ΔI R2 of synchronous fluorescence and CTC concentration in the range of 1–50 μM. The linear relationship of ordinary fluorescence method is ΔI R1 = 0.0665 C CTC + 0.008 (R 2 = 0.997) and a detection limit (LOD) of 0.46 μM is determined at S/N = 3. Similarly, the linear relationship of synchronous fluorescence is ΔI R2 = 0.0196 C CTC + 0.0007 (R 2 = 0.998) and LOD is 0.36 μM. By comparing the results of conventional fluorescence and synchronous fluorescence for detection of CTC, we find that the linear range of conventional fluorescence is larger than that of synchronous fluorescence, but the detection limit and the linear relation of synchronous fluorescence is lower and better than that of conventional fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the CDs-CuInS2/ZnS QDs based fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selectivity of the sensor, common ions, amino acids and other tetracycline drugs with similar structure to CTC were selected as interference substances. Under the same conditions, the selectivity of the constructed detection method to CTC was studied by fluorescence and synchronous fluorescence. The concentration of natural amino acids and common ions is 1 mM, while the concentration of tetracycline which has a similar structure to CTC is 50 μM. The results of the two methods are consistent. In tetracycline drugs (C, OTC, TC and CTC) with similar structure, only CTC can enhance the fluorescence intensity ratio, which indicates that the designed sensor could distinguish CTC from tetracycline classes (Fig. 3C and 3D). To further confirm the selectivity of the probe to the CTC, different ions are detected under the same conditions, such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, SO4 -, NO3 –, and various natural - amino acids. As shown in Fig. 3C and 3D, for the tested ions and amino acids, the fluorescence ratios are not significantly different. These findings show that there is no noticeable interference to CTC determination by the other common ions and other tetracycline drugs and the CDs-QDs sensor had good selectivity to CTC in both fluorescence and synchronous fluorescence modes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CTC in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to further verify the feasibility of this dual-mode fluorescence sensor, it is applied to detect CTC in tap water and milk. CTC with different concentrations (50 μM, 10 μM, 1 μM) are added into tap water and milk, and the recovery is determined by the constructed fluorescent sensor. The recovery is measured by (Detected - Blank) /Added × 100%. As Tables 1 and 2
shown, the recovery rate of fluorescence method is between 101.9% − 107.8% in tap water and 96.7% − 108.8% in milk. In the same way, the recovery of synchronous fluorescence method is in the range of 92.7% to 108.0% in tap water and 99.9% − 104.6% in milk, and the relative standard deviation is less than 5%. Therefore, the constructed dual-mode fluorescent sensor could be used to detect CTC in milk or tap water.

By comparing the results of the two methods, the recovery rate of conventional fluorescence method is higher than that of synchronous fluorescence method. However, false positives may exist in both methods, leading to low recovery, in the process of detecting CTC in milk, due to the existence of some components in milk, the fluorescence intensity of QDs will decrease, leading to the increase of fluorescence intensity ratio, resulting in a certain error in the final result. Compared with other studies (Table 3 ), ordinary fluorescence and synchronous fluorescence methods in this work have a wide range and acceptable detection limit for the detection of CTC.

As Scheme 1 , the prepared CDs have an emission at 438 nm under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. After the addition of QDs, the fluorescence of CDs is quenched. In the presence of CTC, the red emission peak of QDs (664 nm) remains unchanged, while the blue emission peak at 438 nm increases linearly with the concentration of CTC. Therefore, the concentration of CTC is determined according to the change of fluorescence intensity ratio.

As shown in Fig. 4 A, there is a large overlap between the wide absorption of CuInS2/ZnS QDs in the range of 400–600 nm and the emission fluorescence spectrum of CDs [22,23]. Further, we investigated the fluorescence lifetime of CDs with or without QDs. As Fig. 4B shown, the CDs lifetime is 7.12 ns, while the averaged lifetime of CDs after adding CuInS2/ZnS QDs is 6.21 ns. The decrease in lifetime confirms the FRET from the synthesized CD donor to CuInS2/ZnS QD acceptor [24,25]. In addition, we can see from the TEM image (Fig. S4A) that the distance between CDs and CuInS2/ZnS QDs is small. Therefore, FRET between CDs and CuInS2/ZnS QDs results in fluorescence quenching of CDs. With the addition of CTC, the fluorescence of CDs recovers. We compare the infrared spectra of CDs-QDs without and with CTC (Fig. S4B), and it is found that new peaks appear between 1660 cm−1 and 1710 cm−1 with the addition of CTC, which could be attributed to the bond between CTC and CDs-QDs, then increasing the distance between CDs and QDs, thus restoring the fluorescence of CDs.

In this work, a novel fluorometric probe based on CDs and CuInS2/ZnS QDs with a built-in correction that eliminates the environmental effects and increases sensor accuracy to detect CTC. With the addition of CTC, the fluorescence intensity of CDs increases, while that of QDs remains unchanged, leading to fluorescence intensity ratio response toward CTC. CDs-CuInS2/ZnS QDs are particularly attractive as fluorescence sensor of CTC, because the switch of fluorescence signals from “off” to “on” can improve the sensitivity and specificity of detection. In addition, the detection system has been successfully applied to the determination of CTC in milk and tap water, and the preparation is simple and effective. This work provides a new platform for the detection of aureomycin in food, environment and medicine. By comparing the CTC results of conventional fluorescence and synchronous fluorescence, it is found that synchronous fluorescence has a lower detection limit than conventional fluorescence, but conventional fluorescence has a wider linear range than synchronous fluorescence. But in the detection process, the change of synchronous fluorescence is more obvious than that of conventional fluorescence, and the spectral band of synchronous fluorescence is narrower, which can avoid the interference of frequency doubling peak and so on. In conclusion, the two methods have their own advantages. If the linear range is large, the conventional fluorescence method should be chosen. If lower aureomycin content is needed, synchronous fluorescence method is selected.

Xiaoping Chen: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jianwei Lin: Validation. Yafeng Zhuang: Investigation. Siqi Huang: Data curation. Jinghua Chen: Writing – review & editing. Zhizhong Han: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (51602053), Joint Funds for the Innovation of Science and Technology, Fujian Province (2017Y9122), Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2019 J01300), Program for New Century Excellent Talents in Fujian Province University (2018B031).
